sentences.text: Well, I was commanding my own unit as a major, and I'd been doing that since June, 1942.
Ah, well, they gave me a new number then. 140218.
And I had-- still had my identity labels.
I'll show them to you later.

Well, after our battle outside Belsen concentration camp, we headed for the Elbe River, and we fondly believed the war for us would finish when we reached the Elbe and that the Russians would meet us on the Elbe.
So we were all quite pleased when, in actual fact, about the 21st of-- somewhere around about the 21st of April, we actually reached the Elbe opposite Lowenberg.
And we started cleaning ourselves up, and we had inspections and church services.
And then all of a sudden, my colonel grabbed me one day and said, come on, Ted, pick up your map case.
There's a war on.
So we went along to see the commander brigadier, his orders group, and he told us that for political reasons, we had to get to the Baltic.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was a very able soldier, and I admired him intensely.
And we got along like a house on fire.
And I thought when he introduced his orders, for political reasons we've got to get to the Baltic as soon as possible.
There couldn't be a better way of opening up, when I went back to see my chaps, my orders group were the same words.
And so anyway, we-- we had the orders group with the brigadier, and he briefed me for what he wanted.
I raised one or two points on the spot.
He didn't agree with half of them.
However, events proved him to be right.
But then we crossed the River Elbe.
This would be about the 27th of April.
First commando brigade, and I was supporting them with a mounted regiment.
But the infantry who were actually going in was the first commando brigade, which incidentally I should say comprised of four commandos, two army and two royal marine.
Number three commando, which is commanded by a very young chap, and number six commando, which-- his name was Tony Lewis.
He was a Dorset regiment.
And then number six commando, which was also army, which was commanded by Colonel Bartholomew, who is a royal Sussex regiment.
And then the two royal marine commandos, 45 was commanded by Colonel Nichol Gray, a royal marine commando.
And 46 royal marine commando was commanded by Colonel [INAUDIBLE].
And that was the four you might call battalions of infantry.
But of course, they were absolutely elite troops.
Absolutely.
Anyway, we-- we did our attack of the eastern bank of the Elbe was an escarpment, and where we were taking off from was all low lying with osier beds.
You know what I mean, osier beds?
Willows.
Willows for basket making.
And we had quite a struggle getting up this cliff, but we got up it.
And then we raced like hell for the Baltic at Lubeck.
And-- oh, it was very interesting, really.
Prior to doing the assault over the river, we tried to talk the Germans into surrendering to give us this crossing, but the Germans said, no, you pretty well got to fight for it.
Not bad, you know, because they knew they'd lost the war.
Anyway, that night we went over at night, and by early morning next day, we had actually captured the chap who did the parleying with our intelligence officer, and he was in the bag.
And our intelligence officer said, you know, he's a pretty decent chap, and he offered me dinner.
It's the little things.
Anyway, I fell and broke my braces.
And I had get some braces to hitch my trousers up, and I said to one of my signalers, go and find me a pair of braces, Amos.

And he came back with the strongest pair of braces-- this is a little digression-- he came back with the strongest pair of braces I have ever seen, and not without cause, the name on them was Herkules, spelled with a K.
And so I said, these look good, Amos.
He said, yeah, I don't say the poor old bastard will need any more.
So I said to him, why is that?
He said, he's dead.
So I said, well, that's a bit rough justice.
All I asked for was a pair of braces.
Anyway, I was quite pleased when he told me that he was already dead.
He'd been killed in the shell fire.
And so anyway, I-- I took his braces.
I had needed them.
And then we broke out of there, and we went right across to the Baltic at Lubeck.
And we got to Lubeck, and of course, it was absolute chaos.
The German army was trying to surrender, marching down the road in formations.
No arms, mind you, all ready to capitulate, but of course, we had to go up to the Baltic.
And we got to the Baltic, and we sealed off the [INAUDIBLE] peninsula, and that was why we had to get there.
We wanted-- the powers that be, Churchill or somebody-- wanted that closed off.
From whom I'll leave it to your imagination.
And we had an interesting time in Lubeck.
As a matter of fact, a handful of us were the first people into Lubeck, and when we got into the Rathaus Square, they were pulling the swastika off the front of the town hall.
And we all said, schnell, schnell, schnell, you know?
Do it faster, do it faster.
Than we had to go down to the docks, we liberated a camp of French prisoners of war, officer prisoners of war, who we knew we there, and we went and liberated them.
Actually, they'd already liberated themselves.
But I had a very great French friend of mine, and he actually took the liberation of that camp.
And fantastic, he was, absolutely fantastic.
And then we sorted ourselves out.
We established contact with the 6th Airborne Division who were on our right and had gone up to Wismar, and who, in fact, had met the Russians.
So we now had a continuous front on the north.
And then on the 3rd of May, we got orders to exploit to the north towards the Kiel Canal.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so that brings us up to the 3rd of May when we actually got to Neustadt.
I was doing my usual job.
I was with the tac brigade headquarters.
I was the brigadier's gunner.
And we crossed the Autobahn, and the first person I saw as we were crossing the Autobahn was an RF chap.
And he had a lot of gold scrambled egg on his cap.
He was a group captain.
And he said to me, where the hell have you chaps been?
And I said to him, well, we've come a hell of a long way, you know.
And he said, and you're taking your time about it.
He said, I just want to make the point, we've liberated ourselves.
So that was my reception I got at [INAUDIBLE] where they were all RAF.
It was a sort of joke, but you know, I remembered it.
It got locked in up here.
Anyway, we drove farther on north, up the road, and on the right hand side, there was the Lubecker Bucht.
And the-- We could see ships out at sea, some of which were on fire.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We got moving up this road, and the armored cars were in front of us as reconnaissance.
The armored cars would have belonged to the 11th Armored Division because we were all a part of the 8th Corps.
They 8th Corps consisted of the Armored Division and the Commando Brigade.
And the armored cars would go up bounding forward, and we would bound behind them.
And we were going up-- going up this main road to this place called Neustadt.
Never heard of it, you know?
And I saw a group of people in concentration camp uniform.
Small group.
And in fact, if my memory serves me right, it was my men who drew my attention to.
They were in a copse.
So I walked across to this little group of men, half a dozen.
And of course, they were filthy, wet.
And they had swum ashore from ships, obviously.
And I spoke to them, but they didn't make any effort, you know.
They were just frankly frightened.
So I then whipped up my best German.
[GERMAN]
And [INAUDIBLE] boy, said 'Ruskie,' and pointed to the ships.
And so, I said-- And he pointed.
His belly was empty.
I had my ration, so I took my ration out, and I unwrapped it.
And I gave this ration to this Ruskie, and there must have been six people, I suppose, in this party, and this was one ration from me.
And he broke it up and gave each piece.
And it was humiliating.
Then they told me how they'd been treated, and I was livid.
I had no idea that people could do this sort of thing to one another.
And anyway--
How they're being treated.
This is the first time I'd spoken to people in concentration camp uniform.
These were the very first.
And so--
Oh, well, I can't remember the details of it now, they were beaten with rifle butts and all this sort of thing, you know?
And I don't remember the exact phrasing after 50 years, but I do remember that they described it very effectively as they're using the rifle butt like this, and the boot.
And we knew these things, but we didn't-- had no idea that this sort of thing was done.
Women, children.
[INAUDIBLE], if you know French soldiers' catchwords.
They were filthy beyond words, but they'd had the guts and the energy to get ashore.
So whether they were injured or not, you know, they were-- were in a terrible mess.
And anyway, I told them to go back to Lubeck.
I said, there's no good coming with us because we can't carry you, but you must go back to Lubeck.
And then I said, in Lubeck, very soon you'll see a vehicle with a sign, with a shield, and it will say, 'Red Shield.' now that's the Salvation Army welfare truck.
And I said, if you see this truck with a-- or a house or a flag with a shield, and it says on it, 'Red Shield Salvation Army,' go to that vehicle.
They'll feed you.
And off they went.
I got a salute from them, I remember.
And away they went down the road.
But I was livid.
You know, it's dreadful, dreadful.
And then--
They could well have been, but I wouldn't know.
They were just sorry souls from the concentration camp and still wearing the uniform.
The old boy said he was Ruskie, but you can be a Russian Jew, couldn't you?
After all, [INAUDIBLE] Litvinov was one.
And the-- after they went, and I never saw them again.
And we continue on our bounds up this road behind the armored cars, and we got to this place called Neustadt.
And we harbored in the barracks-- not the barracks.
In the market square by the Rathaus, and we knew the war was finished, you know?
The great thing to do now was to avoid getting shot or running over a mine.
I'd lost two men a couple of days before, killed when their [INAUDIBLE] ran over a mine, which upset me.
And we were sitting in the market square, and I was told to stay where I was by my brigadier.
He said, you stay here, Ted, and then I'll know where you are.
And so I stayed.
I did as I was told.
And my driver started tidying up his jeep.
We had a Ford jeep.
And I remember thinking how bloody lucky we were after all the business since September, '39.
It looked as though it was the end of it, and we were still alive.
And I had very few men killed.
A lot of men had been wounded, like myself, but very few men killed.
Very few men killed.
Blown up, ears gone, eyes gone, balls gone, legs gone, but still alive.
And I was congratulating myself on my good luck.
Then all of a sudden, I saw my colonel, who stood about 6 foot 3, and he bellowed across the square, Ted!
And so I did what we always did.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Orders!
Of course, that meant I had to run.
You always doubled for orders, and you never questioned them when you got them.
You had questions at the end, and it was wise to keep the questions to the minimum.
Anyway, he told me that they'd heard that there was a pretty awful chaos in a camp near the docks, and that there's a lot of concentration camp people there.
And that I had to get in there-- he said, actually said to me, I've volunteered to take over cleaning up this mess.
I said, oh, yes.
He said, no, you take your unit in, and you clean it up.
Now that's actually how I went in.
And because I only had my own jeep there then.
You see, the guns would be way behind, and the men.
And so I hurriedly gave my orders group a rendezvous, and then I drove in my jeep to this shambles of a mess near the docks.
And I went down this road, and I just don't know how I got there now, but I remember the first thing I saw were two hospital trains, Wehrmacht hospital trains.
And I realized that they were not in this camp.
They were clear.
They're on the railway, so that's not my headache.
So I drove on, and of course, there's Germans all over the bloody place.
Some armed, some not armed.
And I got into this camp, and I have never seen anything like it in my life.
Now I reckon this would be between 1:00 and 2:00.
It might have been a little before 1:00.
And as I went in through the gates, I realized that, just merely looking at the place, that this had been at one time a military establishment.
Just what sort of, I didn't know then.
And these people had gone-- be pushed into there for convenience sake.
Somebody had to put them somewhere and didn't know where else to put them.
And I noticed, as soon as I got inside this barracks, or as the Germans call it, [GERMAN].
I realized there was a football field there, and this camp was built round this football field.
Not an unusual thing to do, really.
And there were people everywhere in concentration camp kit.
Old clothes which they had picked up from somewhere.
All ages, old woman, old man, babies.
Terrible mess it was.
Terrible mess.
And I was told that the armored car regiment was in there.
The only-- the only bit of the British army which I saw was one officer who was a-- I think he was a captain, and his armored car.
And I said to him, I'd come in to take over this lot.
What can you tell me about it?
He said, not very much.
He said, I know a meal is on its way.
We've got some cooks cooking a meal.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I don't know.
He said, I just don't know.
And he said, I tried to put them in that nationalities, and they won't go into nationalities.
They prefer to be in their camps.
That's why I knew that there was more than one camp.
And so he said, well, I can't tell you anything else.
I don't know anything else.
I just got here.
I said, oh, fair enough.
Because we were young, you know?
Bright, and we-- we had-- we were elan.
You know what I mean, elan?
And so he turned to me.
He said, well, my parting advice to you is if you can sort out this bleeding mess, you deserve a bloody knighthood.
That's what he said to me.
If you can sort out this bleeding mess, you deserve a bloody knighthood.
Now you don't get that said to you every day of the week, so you remember that sort of thing.
And anyway, I drove around, and it was clear to me that it was a Kriegsmarine [GERMAN].
It wasn't Wehrmacht.
It was Kriegsmarine.
And because when I looked in buildings, I saw sectionized pieces of submarine control chambers and that sort of thing.
It'd obviously been very well maintained, but of course all the public utilities had been destroyed.
Water, gas, all that.
And so I did a bit of hard thinking, and I was a professional soldier.
And I got on the blower to my people, and I got my whole group forward, which included my sergeant major.
By this time, I had picked up a little Dutch Jew whose name was Hubert Steinberg, and I spoke to you about him yesterday.
And Hubert came out of the clink, the German civvie clink.
And he'd been in Stutthof concentration camp, and he'd been a prisoner along with his brother and his mom and dad on the [INAUDIBLE], which was one of the burning ships.
And I learned afterwards that he actually saw the SS murder his parents by clubbing them with the butt of the rifle.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said to him, I said, well-- because I took him on as my interpreter, because I had to have him, because there were so many languages, and there were thousands of Russian in the place.
And then my sergeant major appeared, and I said to him, sergeant major, before this camp was used for this job, it had been a very well maintained regular Kriegsmarine training school.
I know that because I've seen the sectionized pieces of U-boats, which you would only have in a training school.

And I said, my belief is that the German armed forces didn't work very different to the British, and if that is so, somewhere close to here there will be the garrison engineer who was responsible for all the engineering services in the camp.
And so I said, now, take my jeep, because he rode a motorbike.
You take my jeep and my interpreter, go to the Rathaus, tell them you want the garrison engineer, and it's up to the Rathaus to find him.
Anyway, he when he came back with-- within an hour with this chap.
They found him.
And of course, he was put in charge of putting the public utilities back to work.
That was important because you had to have water, you have to have light, and you had to have various other functions, like sewage functions.
Because if you didn't, then the lavatories would be blocked, and you'd have a bigger health problem.
His name was Morgan, funnily enough, which is a Welsh name.
Herr Morgan.
And in my memoirs, I pay tribute to him, the work he did, because when the sergeant major brought him in, I spoke to him through my interpreter.
And I said, do you know where all your old man used to live and worked under you here?
And he said, [GERMAN].
And I said, well, bugger off and find them, and bring them back in here.
And so he went off with this jeep, and came back with all these hands.

And then they were told by me, and in no uncertain fashion, that they wouldn't leave the camp until the water was running, and the emergency generators were working, and there was water going into the sewage.
And they were first class.
And I kept them in the camp for five days, and one of them said to me, well, you know, our wives will be getting very worried about us.
And so I said to Hubert Steinberg, I said, now, translate this very carefully.
I said, tell him to look around, and he'll see all the people here in this camp in this dreadful uniform.
And I said, there are a lot of women all over Europe worrying about what's happened to their people.
Don't tell me you're worried, because you're just a few, and your bloody government's caused all this trouble.
And so anyway, that shut him up.
But they all worked very well, as Germans do.
They worked well.
And then when after five days I let them go home, they lived inside the camp.
They ate in the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We eventually got all the people into accommodation in the camp.
Many of them sort of put themselves into accommodation.
But of course, as I told you before, I asked my colonel if there was typhus in the camp, because we knew at Belson there was typhus.
And they laid it on on the 8th Corps very good.
They sent down the equipment to delouse everybody for typhus bugs.
And as I went around-- this was all on the first day-- we got an elementary system of feeding.
The food was all what you might call soup, and everybody had the same.
I mean, there was no particular kosher food for Jewish people or some other sort of thing.
No vegetarians.
They-- they ate what was in the pot.
And the Germans-- of course, it was a German cookhouse, you know.
Service cookhouse with very good equipment.
And the Germans I put on cleaning the place up, because there was excreta everywhere.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

So that's really how we got into the camp, and as I got around and saw the size of the camp and the problems we had-- because we had to escort the Germans, because prisoners were now taken it out on the Germans, but where we were putting Germans on cleaning up, the last thing we wanted them to be interfered with.
So go and take it out on some Germans somewhere else, and leave these alone.
They're doing the cleaning up job.
And the Russians, I mean, the Russians were bloody terrible.
But you know, they'd been treated abominably.
But my colonel gave me free run.
I closed all the bakeries in the town for five days, and all the bread came into the camp.
And then my colonel [INAUDIBLE] my colonel, and he said, I had to release some of it.
And he said, you needn't brother, Ted, I've already done it.
So he released some for the townspeople.
And then, of course, we had to register these people, and the way we did that was very simple.
Each lot of people, they had to appoint their own leader, and he had to put in the ration [INAUDIBLE] so they got their food.
And they registered the names of the people, and we reported these names back to brigade headquarters.
And they were notified to their respective-- it went down the line, down the channel of communication.
But the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was a big job.
My doctor established clinics, and we found a lot of these groups of people, particularly from Southeast Europe.
They had doctors there, and they established their own clinics.
Of course, we provided the drugs.
And then we had to establish bathhouses, and then, of course, clothing.
And I put a levy on the town of Neustadt, and I well remember the figures.
I had-- I asked for 5,000 suits of men's clothes, 5,000 suits of clothes for women, all the children's clothes they can find.
And I'll tell you about that when you're ready.
